title: Week 1 Challenge — Full Company Deep Dive
objectives: Apply annual report, moat and management assessment frameworks to a NZ company
Synthesise multiple analytical dimensions into a coherent investment view
Identify the key investment risks and mitigants for a specific company

vocabulary.definition: A written rationale for why a share is worth buying — covering business model, competitive position, management, key metrics, valuation and risks.
The optimistic scenario (bull) and pessimistic scenario (bear) for a share. Good analysis explicitly considers both before reaching a conclusion.

questions.question: A company's income statement shows Revenue $600M, COGS $360M, Operating expenses $140M, Interest $20M, Tax $22.4M, Net profit $57.6M. What is the net profit margin?
options: 40% — gross margin (revenue minus COGS) divided by revenue
16.7% — operating profit divided by revenue
9.6% — net profit after tax ($57.6M) divided by revenue ($600M)
5.8% — net profit divided by total costs
questions.explanation: Net profit margin = NPAT ÷ Revenue × 100 = $57.6M ÷ $600M × 100 = 9.6%. This measures how much of each dollar of revenue becomes profit after all costs including tax. A 9.6% net margin is modest — typical for logistics, retail or other competitive industries. FPH's net margin is around 16–18%, reflecting stronger pricing power and operating leverage.

questions.question: Mainfreight has an ROE of 33% with a debt-to-equity ratio of 0.3×. A competitor has an ROE of 35% but a D/E of 2.8×. Which company has demonstrated more impressive capital efficiency?
options: The competitor — 35% ROE is simply higher than 33%
They are identical — ROE is the only relevant metric
Mainfreight — its 33% ROE is achieved with minimal leverage, indicating genuine operational excellence. The competitor's 35% ROE is partly a product of aggressive borrowing, which adds financial risk
The competitor — high debt shows confidence in earnings stability
questions.explanation: ROE can be inflated by leverage (the DuPont formula shows ROE = Net Margin × Asset Turnover × Leverage). A company with D/E of 2.8× is borrowing heavily to boost its apparent equity returns. If earnings fall, it faces debt coverage risk. Mainfreight's 33% ROE with minimal debt is genuinely operationally excellent — it earns those returns from business performance, not financial engineering.

questions.question: An annual report reveals the company's CFO resigned last month with no explanation, the external auditor issued an 'emphasis of matter' note on revenue recognition, and accounts receivable jumped 40% despite only 8% revenue growth. What is the most appropriate investor response?
options: Buy more shares — this volatility represents an overreaction and a buying opportunity
Hold and wait — these issues will likely resolve themselves in the next quarter
Investigate deeply before investing — three simultaneous warning signs (key executive departure, audit concern, unusual receivables growth) are significant red flags warranting thorough due diligence
Ignore these factors — financial fundamentals are the only relevant consideration
questions.explanation: Three simultaneous red flags is a serious pattern. CFO departures are concerning (they have the most detailed knowledge of financial health). Auditor 'emphasis of matter' notes are unusual and suggest material accounting concerns. Accounts receivable growing 5× faster than revenue suggests the company may be recognising revenue before it is collected — a classic earnings quality problem. Together, these warrant deep investigation and extreme caution before investing.

questions.question: FPH's competitive moat is best described as:
options: Cost advantage — FPH produces respiratory care equipment more cheaply than competitors
Network effects — FPH becomes more valuable as more hospitals join its customer base

Switching costs combined with intangible assets — hospitals face clinical workflow disruption if they switch, and FPH holds regulatory approvals, clinical relationships and patents that are hard to replicate
Efficient scale — the medical device market is large enough for only one profitable supplier globally
questions.explanation: FPH's primary moat is switching costs — once a hospital's clinical workflows are built around FPH respiratory devices, switching requires retraining staff, clinical risk management, regulatory re-approval processes and potential interruption to patient care. This is reinforced by intangible assets: FPH holds hundreds of patents, has deep clinical relationships with leading respiratory centres globally, and its products have regulatory approvals in over 100 countries that took decades to build.

questions.question: Which capital allocation decision would you rate MOST negatively for long-term shareholders?
options: Paying a fully imputed dividend equal to 80% of free cash flow when no high-return reinvestment is available
Making a large acquisition in a completely unrelated industry at 45× earnings, funded entirely by taking on $800M of new debt
Buying back shares during a market correction at 30% below the company's estimated intrinsic value
Investing $200M in expanding the company's core business at an expected 18% return on invested capital
questions.explanation: Paying 45× earnings for an unrelated acquisition funded by $800M of new debt is one of the worst possible capital allocation outcomes. Research shows diversification acquisitions typically destroy value — management lacks expertise in the new industry, integration is complex, and the price paid is often extreme (45× is very aggressive). Funding it with debt amplifies the risk — if the acquisition underperforms (as most do), the company faces both earnings disappointment and debt service pressure. Options A, C and D all represent sensible capital deployment.

activities.title: Full Company Deep Dive — Mainfreight Analysis
activities.setup:
Mainfreight Limited (NZX: MFT) is a global logistics company founded in Auckland in 1978. It operates freight forwarding, warehousing and supply chain services across NZ, Australia, USA, Europe and Asia.

Selected financial data (FY2024):
Revenue: $5,100 million
Gross profit: $1,530 million
Net profit after tax: $300 million
Shares on issue: 108.5 million
Shareholders' equity: $900 million
Total debt: $270 million
Capital expenditure: $320 million
Operating cash flow: $490 million
Annual dividend: $1.50/share
Share price: $68.50
52-week range: $58.40 – $82.10

Management notes:
— CEO Don Braid has led the company since 2007. Revenue has grown from $1.5B to $5.1B under his tenure.
— Braid owns approximately 6% of all shares (~6.5 million shares).
— No acquisitions in the past 3 years — management describes acquisitions as 'very occasional and small.'
— The company has no independent directors on the remuneration committee — a governance concern raised by some proxy advisors.

Competitive position:
— Mainfreight has 350+ branch locations globally
— No single customer represents more than 2% of revenue
— Competitors include DHL, FedEx, Toll and Linfox in key markets
activities.challenge:
Calculate and analyse:
1. EPS, P/E ratio and dividend yield
2. Gross margin and net profit margin
3. ROE and D/E ratio
4. Free cash flow and dividend coverage ratio (FCF ÷ dividends paid)

Assess:
5. What type of competitive moat does Mainfreight have? Is it widening or narrowing?
6. Evaluate management quality using three specific pieces of evidence from the data.
7. Identify two green flags and two red flags in the data provided.
8. Write a 5-sentence investment opinion: is Mainfreight a buy, hold or sell at $68.50?
hints: Q4: Dividends paid = shares × DPS = 108.5M × $1.50. Dividend coverage = FCF ÷ dividends paid.
Q5: Think about Mainfreight's 350+ branch global network and what this means for a competitor trying to replicate it.
Q8: Consider valuation (P/E vs historical average and sector), management alignment, moat strength and current technical position (below 52-week high).
activities.solution:
1. EPS = $300M ÷ 108.5M = $2.765/share. P/E = $68.50 ÷ $2.765 = 24.8×. Dividend yield = $1.50 ÷ $68.50 × 100 = 2.19%.

2. Gross margin = $1,530M ÷ $5,100M × 100 = 30%. Net profit margin = $300M ÷ $5,100M × 100 = 5.9%.

3. ROE = $300M ÷ $900M × 100 = 33.3%. D/E = $270M ÷ $900M = 0.30×.

4. FCF = $490M − $320M = $170M. Dividends paid = 108.5M × $1.50 = $162.75M. Dividend coverage = $170M ÷ $162.75M = 1.04×. FCF barely covers dividends — the company is investing heavily in growth (high capex) which compresses near-term FCF relative to earnings.

5. Mainfreight's moat is primarily cost advantage (scale) combined with switching costs (deeply embedded in customer supply chains). With 350+ global branches, Mainfreight's network density allows more freight per truck per route than smaller competitors — a structural cost advantage. Moat appears to be widening: revenue grew from $1.5B to $5.1B under the current CEO, each new geography makes the network more useful for international freight customers.

6. Management quality assessment:
(a) Track record: Revenue grew 3.4× and net profit grew substantially under Braid since 2007. This is exceptional long-term performance. (b) Skin in the game: Braid personally owns ~6% of all shares worth over $400M — extraordinary personal alignment with shareholders. (c) Capital allocation discipline: No acquisitions in 3 years, preferring organic investment. High capex of $320M suggests meaningful reinvestment in the business at expected returns well above cost of capital.

7. Green flags: (a) CEO owns 6% of shares — very strong alignment. (b) Revenue diversification — no single customer more than 2% of revenue, reducing customer concentration risk.
Red flags: (a) No independent directors on remuneration committee — governance concern. (b) Dividend coverage of 1.04× is very thin — any earnings shortfall could require a dividend cut or funding dividends from debt.

8. Investment opinion (HOLD/BUY on weakness):
Mainfreight is an exceptional NZ business with a 33% ROE, widening global moat and outstanding management alignment under Don Braid. At 24.8× P/E the valuation is not cheap for a logistics company (sector typically trades 15–20×), reflecting the premium the market rightly assigns to Mainfreight's track record and management quality. The current price at $68.50 — roughly 17% below the 52-week high of $82.10 — has partially corrected this premium. A stronger buy case would emerge if the price falls toward the $58–62 range where the P/E drops to ~21–22× and the yield approaches 2.5%. Current recommendation: hold existing positions and accumulate on weakness below $62.

activities.title: What Made This Analysis Difficult?
activities.prompt: Which part of the Mainfreight analysis did you find hardest — the financial calculations, the moat assessment, the management evaluation, or the final investment opinion? Write three sentences about what made it difficult and what additional information would have helped you form a stronger view.
activities.nceaLink: Extra Learning — real company analysis is genuinely difficult. Professional analysts spend weeks on each company. The goal is building a framework, not achieving certainty.
